              /home/archon/proj/Cech-Cohomology/.archon/logs/iter-071/effort-breaker-corecomm071-directive.md

            DIRECTIVE:
            # effort-breaker directive — slug corecomm071

## Target
`lem:coreIso_comm` in `blueprint/src/chapters/Cohomology_CechHigherDirectImage.tex`
(Lean: `AlgebraicGeometry.coreIso_comm`, `AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/CechSectionIdentification.lean`
line ~1468, the file's highest-effort open sorry, effort 2242). Its deps
(`lem:coreIso_obj_iso`, `lem:coverInterOpen_inf_distrib`, `lem:section_cech_objd_apply`,
`lem:section_cech_product_equiv`) are all DONE — it is the live frontier bottleneck.

## Granularity
Fine — one mathematical claim per sub-lemma. A prior coarser break (iter-067: the
lattice/objIso/comm 3-chain) landed the first two; the remaining `coreIso_comm` piece stalled with a
precise recorded stuck point. Break along that stuck point.

## Proof structure (cut at these two seams)

The square to prove: \((\mathrm{objIso}\,p).\mathrm{hom} \cdot d^p_{\check{\mathcal C}(\mathcal U')}
= d^p_{(G_V\circ\Psi)\check{\mathcal C}(\mathcal U)} \cdot (\mathrm{objIso}\,(p+1)).\mathrm{hom}\),
where both differentials are alternating coface sums (`AlternatingCofaceMapComplex.objD`,
\(d = \sum_k (-1)^k \delta_k\)).

**Seam S1 — the per-coface square (the mathematical content).** For each coface index
\(k \le p+1\): the evaluated push–pull coface matches the section-Čech face restriction at \(k\),
\[(\mathrm{objIso}\,p).\mathrm{hom} \cdot \delta^{\mathrm{sec}}_k
 = G_V(\Psi(\delta^{\mathrm{nerve}}_k)) \cdot (\mathrm{objIso}\,(p+1)).\mathrm{hom},\]
both sides being induced by the SAME inclusion of intersection opens
\(\bigl(\bigwedge_j (\mathrm{coverOpen}\,\mathcal U\,((\sigma\circ\delta^k)\,j)\bigr) \sqcap V
\le \bigl(\bigwedge_j \mathrm{coverOpen}\,\mathcal U\,(\sigma'\,j)\bigr) \sqcap V\). Route: check on
elements through `sectionCechProductEquiv` (lem:section_cech_product_equiv); per product leg \(\sigma\)
the LHS is the face restriction `sectionCechFaceRestr` and the RHS unwinds through the leg structure of
`objIso` — `pushPull_eval_prod_iso` (Stub 4) factoring through `pushPull_sigma_iso` (Stub 2),
`PreservesProduct.iso`, and `pushPull_leg_sections` (Stub 3) — to the restriction of \(\mathcal F\)
along the same open inclusion. If this single claim is still large, ITERATE: split off the
"one product leg" naturality (the projection of the RHS onto the \(\sigma\)-component equals the
\(\mathcal F\)-restriction along the face inclusion) as its own sub-lemma.

**Seam S2 — the alternating-sum bookkeeping (the recorded Lean blocker).** Given S1 per coface,
sum: push the additive functors \(\Psi\), \(G_V\) and the leading/trailing compositions through
\(\sum_k (-1)^k \delta_k\). The iter-067 prover recorded that the categorical sum route
(`Preadditive.comp_sum`/`Functor.map_sum`) clashes with the bundled `AddCommGrpCat`-hom representation
of `objD`; the recommended shape is ELEMENTWISE — state S2 as an identity of group elements via
`sectionCech_objD_apply` (lem:section_cech_objd_apply), so both sides become pointwise finite sums
\(\sum_k (-1)^k\,(\text{restriction})\) and S1 is applied summand-by-summand under `Finset.sum_congr`.

## Output
2–3 `\uses`-chained sub-lemma blocks (statement + informal proof each), with fresh `\label`s
(suggest `lem:coreIso_comm_coface`, `lem:coreIso_comm_sum` — adjust as natural) and fresh `\lean{}`
names you must verify are FREE in the project (e.g. `AlgebraicGeometry.coreIso_comm_coface`);
rewrite `lem:coreIso_comm`'s proof sketch to consume them. Statement-level `\uses` discipline
(leandag reads statement `\uses` only). NO `\leanok`.


            Report: /home/archon/proj/Cech-Cohomology/.archon/task_results/effort-breaker-corecomm071.md

## Protected by the mathematician (`archon-protected.yaml`)

The mathematician owns everything below; respect every rule. Do NOT set or accept an objective that requires violating one.

**Frozen signatures** (you MAY fill the proof body / close a `sorry`, but never rename / re-type / reorder args / weaken hypotheses):

content: I'll start by reading my instructions and the target context.

1	---
2	name: effort-breaker
3	description: Split ONE high-effort blueprint declaration into a \uses-linked chain of smaller, easier sub-lemmas — each with its own statement, informal proof, and \uses — so the prover formalizes small pieces instead of one large goal. On-demand; iterative (re-dispatch to break a still-hard sub-lemma further, down to sentence level).
4	write_domain: "blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex"
5	read_only: false
6	can_spawn: true
7	default_enabled: false
8	dispatcher_notes: |
9	  - Dispatch me against ONE target declaration that is too big to formalize in
10	    one go — a high-`effort_local` node, or one the progress-critic flags as a
11	    repeated blocker. I am most valuable on a **frontier** node (deps already
12	    done): it is the live bottleneck, and decomposing it exposes small ready
13	    pieces next iter.
14	  - Name the target label in `## Target`, and say **how aggressively to break**
15	    in `## Granularity`: "one level" (the proof's main steps) by default, or
16	    "fine — one mathematical claim per lemma" when a prior, coarser break still
17	    left a hard piece. Decomposition is ITERATIVE and has no floor: if a break
18	    doesn't drop the difficulty enough, re-dispatch me on the still-hard
19	    sub-lemma asking me to break it further (sentence-by-sentence, like the
20	    fine-grained prover). Do not pre-empt this — just break, see if it helped,
21	    break more if not.
22	  - Give me the proof sketch / reference structure to cut along in
23	    `## Proof structure` so I split at real mathematical seams, not arbitrary
24	    points.
25	  - Do NOT dispatch me at an already-done node (`\leanok` / `\mathlibok`) — there
26	    is nothing to break. Do NOT dispatch me at an ∞-effort node (no informal
27	    proof to decompose) — send a dag-walker / blueprint-writer to write the
28	    proof first, then me to break it.
29	  - **Never instruct me to add `\leanok`** (sync_leanok's job). `\mathlibok` only
30	    on genuine Mathlib anchors.
31	---
32	
33	# Effort Breaker
34	
35	You take **one high-effort blueprint declaration** and split it into a `\uses{}`-linked chain of **smaller, easier sub-lemmas**, so the prover formalizes a sequence of small goals instead of one large one. The target's proof becomes "by `L1`, `L2`, …, `L_n`", each `L_i` is a new block with its own statement, informal proof, and `\uses{}`, and each `L_i` is strictly easier than the original.
36	
37	You decompose DOWN (one node → many smaller ones). This is the complement of the dag-walker (which fills the cone UP). You do not invent new mathematics — you re-express the target's existing proof as a chain of named intermediate results.
38	
39	## Directive Format
40	
41	```markdown
42	# Effort Breaker Directive
43	
44	## Slug
45	<slug>
46	
47	## Target
48	<the blueprint label to break, e.g. thm:flattening_stratification_exists>
49	
50	## Granularity
51	<"one level" (the proof's main steps) | "fine — one mathematical claim per
52	 lemma" (when a coarser break already happened and a piece is still hard)>
53	
54	## Proof structure
55	<the seams to cut along: the steps of the target's informal proof, or the
56	 structure of the source proof. The dispatcher gives you this so you split at
57	 real mathematical boundaries.>
58	
59	## Strategy context
60	<the slice of STRATEGY.md that matters — what this result supports. You do NOT
61	 read STRATEGY.md yourself.>
62	
63	## References
64	- <path/to/reference.md>: <which proof / steps live here>
65	```
66	
67	## How you break
68	
69	1. Read the target block on disk and inspect it: `archon dag-query node --node <target> --json` (effort, deps, used-by). `archon` is on PATH; `--json` is parseable (banner on stderr).
70	2. Read the target's informal proof (and the source proof it cites, per the directive's references). Identify the **steps** — each self-contained claim the proof establishes on the way to the conclusion.
71	3. For each step, author a new sub-lemma block in the appropriate chapter:
72	   - a `\label{lem:<descriptive>}` and a `\lean{...}` hint (the Lean name the prover will use; name it by convention if the directive doesn't give one, and note it for the plan agent),
73	   - the **statement** of that step in the project's notation,
74	   - an informal `\begin{proof}` for the step with `\uses{...}` naming its own dependencies (existing blocks, Mathlib anchors, or earlier sub-lemmas in this chain),
75	   - citation discipline (below) when the step derives from a source.
76	4. **Rewrite the target's proof** to invoke the chain: a short proof body that combines `L1 … L_n`, with `\uses{lem:L1, …, lem:L_n}` (plus any direct deps that remain). The target keeps its statement and `\lean{}` unchanged.
77	5. Each `L_i` must be **strictly smaller** than the target (smaller proof, fewer moving parts). If a step is still large, either break it into sub-steps now (at fine granularity) or flag it in the report so the dispatcher can re-break it next round.
78	
79	Granularity is the directive's call. "One level" = the proof's main steps. "Fine" = one mathematical assertion per lemma — the limit is the same as the fine-grained prover: a lemma so small its proof is a single move. There is no floor; if a break didn't help, the next dispatch breaks further.
80	
81	## Markers and citation (same rules as every blueprint agent)
82	
83	- **Never add `\leanok`** — it is the deterministic `sync_leanok` phase's job.
84	- **`\mathlibok` only on a genuine Mathlib dependency anchor** — when a step is a result Mathlib already provides, write it as an anchor (`\lean{<real Mathlib decl>}`, `\mathlibok`, `\textit{Provided by Mathlib.}`, no proof) rather than re-proving it. Mark `\mathlibok` ONLY when the statement genuinely exists in Mathlib in the form you wrote — a wrong anchor is worse than leaving the step to prove.
85	- **Citation discipline (hard rule):** every block derived from a source needs `% SOURCE:` (`<pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`), a verbatim original-language `% SOURCE QUOTE:` copied from that local file, a `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` before the proof env when applicable, and a visible `\textit{Source: ...}` first line. Never write these from memory; if you lack the local source, spawn a `reference-retriever` (Bash, blocking — needs `references/**` in your write-domain), wait, read the new file, then write the cited block. Never substitute a paraphrase or a remembered quote.
86	
87	## Rules
88	
89	- **Stay in your write-domain** (`blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`). No `.lean`, no `content.tex`, no `macros/`. A needed macro is a "Notes" item.
90	- **Keep the chapter valid LaTeX** (matched `\begin`/`\end`, balanced braces).
91	- **Mathematical prose, not Lean syntax.** No tactics, no project-history narrative.
92	- **Preserve the target's statement and `\lean{}`.** You re-express its *proof* as a chain; you do not weaken or restate the theorem. (If the target's statement looks wrong, do NOT silently fix it — flag it under "Notes" as a strategy item.)
93	- **Conserve the mathematics.** The chain must actually prove the target — every gap the original proof crossed is covered by some `L_i`. Don't drop a hard step by relabelling it; a step you cannot decompose or prove is reported, not hidden.
94	- **Verify with the graph.** After breaking, run `archon dag-query node --node <target>` and `archon dag-query ancestors --node <target>`: the target's effort should drop, the new `L_i` should appear, and there must be no broken `\uses{}`. Fix and re-check.
95	
96	## Workflow
97	
98	1. Read the directive and the target block on disk.
99	2. Read the references the directive names; identify the proof's steps.
100	3. Author the sub-lemma chain (statement + proof + `\uses` for each), bottom-up so later lemmas can `\uses{}` earlier ones.
101	4. Rewrite the target's proof to invoke the chain.
102	5. For any step needing a source you lack, spawn a `reference-retriever`, wait, read the file, then write the cited block.
103	6. Re-query the graph; confirm the target's effort dropped and no `\uses{}` broke.
104	7. Write your report.
105	
106	## Logging
107	
108	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/effort-breaker-<slug>.md` (or the nested `task_results/<parent-slug>/...` path your invocation names).
109	
110	```markdown
111	# Effort Breaker Report
112	
113	## Slug
114	<slug>
115	
116	## Target
117	<label>
118	
119	## Status
120	<COMPLETE — target re-expressed as a chain | PARTIAL — a step could not be broken/proved (listed below)>
121	
122	## Effort before → after
123	- target `effort_local`: <before> → <after>
124	- sub-lemmas added: <count>
125	
126	## Chain added (target ← L_n ← … ← L1)
127	- `\label{lem:L1}` `\lean{...}` in `chapters/<slug>.tex` — <statement, one line> (effort ≈ <small>)
128	- `\label{lem:L2}` … — <statement> (\uses{lem:L1})
129	- ...
130	- Target `<label>` proof rewritten: `\uses{lem:L1, …, lem:L_n}`.
131	
132	## Still hard (re-break candidates)
133	- `lem:Lk` — still large; re-dispatch the breaker at finer granularity, OR <why it's irreducible>.
134	
135	## Could not decompose (strategy items)
136	- <step> — <why: genuinely atomic / novel / statement looks wrong>.
137	
138	## References consulted
139	- `references/<file>.md` — <verbatim content taken>.
140	
141	## Notes for dispatcher
142	- `\lean{}` names I assigned by convention (confirm/scaffold): <list>.
143	- needed macros / cross-chapter concerns.
144	```
145	
146	## Return Value
147	
148	Your final assistant message: one line — `<slug>: COMPLETE | PARTIAL — <one-sentence outcome>` — plus the path to your full report. Keep it short; the dispatcher reads the report.
149

Let me read the project AGENTS.md and the target blueprint block.

1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Under Claude Code, skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Under Claude Code, rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — under Claude Code this is installed as the `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source), providing slash commands `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc. Under other harnesses the slash-command surface is absent; the underlying skill scripts are still on disk under `.claude/skills/` and can be run directly from the shell.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts — that path is the same whatever harness you run under. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) surface as `mcp__*` tools: under Claude Code they are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json`; under other harnesses the loop wires the same servers in at launch.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned agent reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**A dispatch is a BLOCKING Bash call — wait by staying inside it.** `archon-subagent.py` is synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and returns only once the child finishes and its report is on disk). A long dispatch may be auto-backgrounded with a task ID — that's fine; wait for that task's result before acting. **Do NOT use the `Monitor` tool to wait** — it is non-blocking (returns immediately, "keep working"), so your turn ends and the subagents are abandoned mid-run. **Do NOT use foreground `sleep` / `until … ; do sleep … ; done`** — the harness blocks `sleep`. To run independent subagents **in parallel**, put all their dispatches in **one Bash call**, each backgrounded with `&`, ending with `wait` — e.g. `python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py --name A … & python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py --name B … & wait` — which blocks until all finish (the whole wave as one blocking dispatch). Serialize only subagents that write the **same file** (separate sequential calls). The rule never changes: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required.
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `ARCHON_MEMORY.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read (edits via `archon discuss`) |
57	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
58	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
60	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
61	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
62	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
65	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
66	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
67	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
68	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | read | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
69	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
70	| `TO_USER.md` | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | read |
71	
72	### Subagent permissions
73	
74	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
75	
76	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
77	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
78	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
79	
80	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
81	
82	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper.
83	
84	## Protected Declarations
85	
86	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
87	
88	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
89	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
90	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
91	
92	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
93	
94	Two semantic markers:
95	
96	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
97	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
98	
99	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
100	
101	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake build <module>` (it uses `lake build`, not `lake env lean <file>`, so dependencies are built and missing-import failures aren't spurious), and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents should NOT add or remove `\leanok` as routine bookkeeping — leave it to the sync. The one exception is the review agent: if it is **certain** the sync got a marker wrong (a clearly-finished proof left unmarked, or a marker present on something incomplete), it may manually correct it, but must explicitly surface and justify that override in its summary (see `.archon/prompts/review.md`).
102	
103	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
104	
105	## User Interaction
106	
107	The user provides hints in two places:
108	
109	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
110	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
111	
112	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
113	
114	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
115	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true or important at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
116	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.
117	
118	## Agent Roles
119	
120	### Plan Agent
121	
122	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
123	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
124	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
125	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
126	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
127	- Curate `ARCHON_MEMORY.md` — the condensed, cross-iteration knowledge file injected (read-only) into every agent's prompt. Keep only durable facts that would surprise an agent reading the code fresh (dead-end tactics, files not to touch, Mathlib gap coordinates, protected invariants). Hard limits: ~10 bullets / ~600 chars; prune before adding. Do NOT duplicate what's obvious from the code or PROGRESS.md.
128	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
129	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
130	
131	### Prover Agent
132	
133	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
134	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
135	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
136	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
137	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
138	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
139	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
140	
141	### Subagents
142	
143	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
144	
145	### Review Agent
146	
147	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
148	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
149	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
150	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
151	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Normally leave `\leanok` to the deterministic `sync_leanok` phase; only override a marker the sync got demonstrably wrong, and justify it in your summary.
152	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
153	
154	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
155	
156	These run automatically between phases:
157	
158	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
159	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
160	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.
161

8761	  \mathcal{F}\bigr)\), where \(\mathcal{U}'\) is the restricted family \(U'_i =
8762	  \operatorname{coverOpen}\mathcal{U}\,i \cap V\).

8768	  \Gamma(\operatorname{coverInterOpen}\mathcal{U}\,\sigma \cap V, \mathcal{F})\). Post-compose
8769	  with the product reindexing \(\prod_\sigma \Gamma(\cdot, \mathcal{F})\) along the open-meet
8770	  identity \(\operatorname{coverInterOpen}\mathcal{U}\,\sigma \cap V = \bigcap_k
8771	  (\operatorname{coverOpen}\mathcal{U}\,(\sigma k)\cap V)\) of
8772	  Lemma~\ref{lem:coverInterOpen_inf_distrib} — factorwise the \(\operatorname{eqToIso}\) on the
8773	  section group \(\Gamma(\cdot, \mathcal{F})\) obtained by applying \(\Gamma(\cdot, \mathcal{F})\)
8774	  to that equality of opens. The composite lands on the degree-\(p\) term \(\prod_\sigma
8775	  \Gamma(\bigcap_k(\operatorname{coverOpen}\mathcal{U}\,(\sigma k)\cap V), \mathcal{F})\) of
8776	  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}', \mathcal{F})\), since for the restricted family
8777	  \(U'_{\sigma k} = \operatorname{coverOpen}\mathcal{U}\,(\sigma k)\cap V\) the section
8778	  {\v C}ech term at \(\sigma\) is exactly \(\Gamma(\bigcap_k U'_{\sigma k}, \mathcal{F})\).
8779	\end{proof}

8786	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.coreIso_comm}

8788	    lem:coverInterOpen_inf_distrib}

8790	  isomorphisms \(\mathrm{objIso}\,p\) intertwine the two differentials:
8791	  \[
8792	    (\mathrm{objIso}\,p).\mathrm{hom} \cdot
8793	      d^{p}_{\check{\mathcal{C}}(\mathcal{U}')}
8794	      \;=\;
8795	    d^{p}_{(G_V\circ\Psi)\check{\mathcal{C}}(\mathcal{U})} \cdot
8796	      (\mathrm{objIso}\,(p+1)).\mathrm{hom}.
8797	  \]
8798	  Consequently \(\mathrm{coreIso} := \operatorname{isoOfComponents}(\mathrm{objIso},\,\cdot)\)
8799	  is an isomorphism of cochain complexes \((G_V\circ\Psi)\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U},
8800	  \mathcal{F}) \cong \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}', \mathcal{F})\).
8801	\end{lemma}
8802	\begin{proof}

8804	    lem:coverInterOpen_inf_distrib}
8805	  Read the section-complex differential \(d^p_{\check{\mathcal{C}}(\mathcal{U}')}\) through the
8806	  product equivalence \(\operatorname{sectionCechProductEquiv}\)
8807	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_product_equiv}): by Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_objd_apply} it is
8808	  the alternating sum of presheaf face restrictions \(\sum_i (-1)^i\,
8809	  \operatorname{sectionCechFaceRestr}(\sigma, i)\), each restriction being induced by the
8810	  inclusion of the deleted-face intersection into the full intersection of the restricted opens
8811	  \(U'_{\sigma k}\). The differential \(d^p_{(G_V\circ\Psi)\check{\mathcal{C}}(\mathcal{U})}\) is
8812	  the evaluation at \(V\) of the {\v C}ech-nerve coface maps of the backbone complex, assembled
8813	  from the same push--pull restriction comparisons. Under the degreewise identification of
8814	  Lemma~\ref{lem:coreIso_obj_iso} — the evaluated push--pull product iso reindexed by the
8815	  open-meet identity (Lemma~\ref{lem:coverInterOpen_inf_distrib}) — each coface of
8816	  \((G_V\circ\Psi)\check{\mathcal{C}}(\mathcal{U})\) becomes the corresponding presheaf face
8817	  restriction, because both are induced by the \emph{same} inclusion of intersection opens
8818	  \(\bigcap_k(\operatorname{coverOpen}\mathcal{U}\,(\sigma k)\cap V)\). Hence the two alternating
8819	  sums agree termwise and the square commutes; reusing the alternating-sum bookkeeping of
8820	  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_objd_apply} discharges every degree at once.
8821	
8822	  The degree-\(0\) compatibility datum \(h_{\mathrm{compat}}\) of
8823	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cechSection_complex_iso} is the bottom-degree instance of this same match: the
8824	  evaluated {\v C}ech augmentation \(G_V(\Psi(\operatorname{cechAugmentation}))\), read through
8825	  the \(p=0\) object iso \(\mathrm{objIso}\,0\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:coreIso_obj_iso} at \(p=0\)), is
8826	  the restriction-product map \(\varepsilon\colon t \mapsto (t|_{U'_i})_i\) on the nose, because
8827	  both are induced by the inclusions \(\operatorname{coverOpen}\mathcal{U}\,i \cap V
8828	  \hookrightarrow V\). The prover therefore discharges \(h_{\mathrm{compat}}\) alongside this
8829	  lemma, as its \(p=0\) case, rather than as a separate obligation.
8830	\end{proof}

8834	  \label{def:sectionCechAugV}
8835	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionCechAugV}

8837	  \textit{Project-local.}
8838	  Fix an open \(V \subseteq X\). The \emph{canonical augmentation}
8839	  \[
8840	    \varepsilon_{\mathrm{can}} : \Gamma(V, \mathcal{F}) \longrightarrow
8841	      \prod_i \Gamma(U_i \cap V, \mathcal{F})
8842	  \]
8843	  of the concrete section {\v C}ech complex over the restricted family
8844	  \(U'_i = \operatorname{coverOpen}\mathcal{U}\,i \cap V\) is the evaluation at \(V\) of the {\v C}ech
8845	  augmentation \(\mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{C}^0\) of Definition~\ref{def:cech_augmentation},
8846	  transported across the degree-\(0\) object isomorphism of Lemma~\ref{lem:coreIso_obj_iso}.
8847	  Concretely it is the composite
8848	  \(G_V\bigl(\Psi(\operatorname{cechAugmentation})\bigr) \cdot (\mathrm{objIso}\,0).\mathrm{hom}\),
8849	  where \(\Psi = \operatorname{forget}\cdot\operatorname{restrictScalars}(\mathrm{id})\) is the
8850	  push--pull adapter, \(G_V = \operatorname{toPresheaf}\cdot\operatorname{ev}_V\) is the
8851	  section-at-\(V\) functor, and \(\mathrm{objIso}\,0\) is the degree-\(0\) instance of
8852	  Lemma~\ref{lem:coreIso_obj_iso}. It carries no free parameter: this is the unique augmentation
8853	  for which the comparisons below hold.
8854	\end{definition}

8857	[The canonical section augmentation kills the first differential]
